data_source: ConocoPhillips FY2007 10-K, SEC EDGAR; Berkshire 2008 annual report; 2015 Berkshire AGM Q&A

g4_revenue_trend.description: Revenue growth into 2007 was driven by commodity pricing and production, not by a structurally worsening business.

g5_gross_margin_trend.description: No clear two-year structural gross-margin collapse before the purchase; the real problem was cyclicality and capital intensity.

g7_moat.description: ConocoPhillips owned valuable assets, but its economics still depended primarily on oil and gas prices. It could not be described as a business competitors could not replicate in 10 years; the product remained a commodity and the returns were cycle-led rather than moat-led.

control_groups.conclusion: PASS — same commodity exposure and no Buffett-style moat despite better scale.
PASS — similarly tied to oil prices and capital intensity, without a durable moat.

buffett_action: Berkshire built a roughly $7B ConocoPhillips position around the 2008 period.
actual_result: Buffett later called the timing a mistake made near the oil-price peak; the investment was not a Berkshire-quality win.
key_insight: Peak-cycle commodity profits can make a stock look optically cheap while still failing the moat and cash-conversion test.
